item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The thing that seems missing is containment procedure for the desert landing spot. Sure it is remote, but it woukd seem to me that it should be, at least, a boring punishment post. Even when the science lab isn't manifested, there is still a chance of other strange events occurring. Also, what happens to the matter that used to occupy the space the science lab, and it's foundation, instantly relocate? What about organic matter that is in the desert while it disappears, what would a video camera put there record during the science lab's occupation? Inquiring minds want to know.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: In the second paragraph, the second mention of Area 216 is mislabeled as Area 16. Other than that typo, I like this. The tone feels to be even and professional throughout. I am curious if the blackness had a cauterizing effect on the surviving janitor.

item.title: know this is old thread of old, but
item.content:encoded:
Special Containment Procedure

Y U singular? Nevah seen befo!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I like the article, I like the concept, but I cannot in good conscience upvote until you reformat the links into something more professional. Right now they're done in Wikipedia style. No.

(Fix that and I will upvote with the force of a great typhoon.)

edit: The same goes for the other two related skips. I want to upvote all three of them — I really do! — but the wiki-style links throw me way off.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: See, that's the thing about expungement. There's nothing the should be redacted, only things that shouldn't be.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
centered on 25.2745° N, 111.2037° W.

This is a sensitive info, in my opinion. It should be redacted.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Don't like how the links to the related scps are presented. Works fine for a story or wiki entry, but not the right tone for a scientific report. To tired/time constrained to do it myself, but how does some of the Class of '76 articles handle it?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Really interesting, especially given the crosslinking with other new skips at the end.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Crosslinking as a crutch between two unrelated articles is discouraged. The creation of meaningful links between articles is totally fine.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
First of all, if a newbie posted three SCPs and they stuck, it wouldn't matter if they were posted ten minutes apart or three years apart. The member page would be earned regardless.

Second of all, you might be surprised to find I had a couple of things already.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I honestly thought Isla Santa Cruz counted as part of the peninsula, even without being contiguous with it. But that was kind of pointless, and I didn't realize I literally missed the point I was aiming for by seven hundred meters. I moved west a smidge. I also had trouble with significant digits with regards to coordinate points; the coordinates I looked up for a spot on Isla Santa Cruz had about seven decimal points, and I just kind of, um, rounded to four. This is apparently Not a Smart Thing to Do. Good catch.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It most certainly is. Think of what the room you're in right now would look like if it was detached from the rest of the building and all the walls were completely, perfectly transparent. The walls are back in Dresden; what's in Mexico is the content of the room without the walls.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Since the walls don't exist when the room is at the B site, I assume they're permeable. A assume that any object placed across the threshold is severed when the room is returned to site A?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
So, the coordinates given in the article for the location of 1380-B do not match the description of that location. The coordinates denote a point ~700m east of the coast of the Isla Santa Cruz (not the same as Santa Cruz Island that is a part of Galápagos Islands), which is in turn rather far away from Baja California, being located almost in the middle of Baja California Sur.

nitpicks for the nitpick god, etc.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Huh, that's kinda neat.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
There is no policy against crosslinking, in general. Like many things you can do when writing an SCP, if you do it badly, you'll get downvoted.

Skara Brae did a little group of related SCPs too…see SCP-1928.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I do see a common element in their affects. Namely, the blackness where the science room was when it dissapears and the blackness in the windows of the buses. Could be that 1380 was a botched prototype for this effect or something.
But this is just my hypothesis.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Eh, I've heard that crossovers with your own SCPs is allowed.

But I just realized, could this be his bid to get a member page in one fell swoop?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is the weakest out of the three, but it still holds up fairly well on its own.

Vincent Sender

I think I see what you did there.

Out of curiosity, is there supposed to be a concrete explanation for why these SCPs have the effects they have, or are the effects just supposed to be random and bizarre?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Crosslinking is generally discouraged, as it a) creates confusion for the reader, forcing them to jump around between multiple articles and b) is sometimes used as a crutch for less interesting articles. I'm not sure what the policy is on crosslinking your own stuff, though.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Wow, three SCPs that are connected to each other somewhat coincidentally and created at the same time.
This seems to be something new, but I'm new here. Is this a unique occurance?

Also, pretty good work on all of them.
